notes: Two-rule fixture: (1) TQ-1 loh sentence-final discourse particle; (2) TQ-3 gender-neutral 'dia → they' default (Indonesian 'dia' is gender-unspecified; English defaults to singular-they rather than guessing he/she). 'loh' here is information-sharing — the speaker is making sure the listener has the date/timing fact. English 'you know' preserves the conversational nudge. VERIFY WITH GIRLFRIEND: in case the listener already knew, 'loh' can also read as gentle correction ('actually, they're coming tomorrow') — Story 3.2b may add a sub-rule for that variant. Sundanese-context note: 'dia' has no Sundanese equivalent register split (Sundanese uses 'manehna' which is gendered), so this fixture stays Indonesian-source.
